text: Implement a minimal first-day TYF book-start path: create and open a work, create author-owned source/interview prompts, capture provenance-safe source/voice/claim/question notes, never write manuscript content except through tyf write --confirm, and make the repo valid as a Codex plugin for immediate Codex use.

TYF should be maximally transparent like SOLO: expose its reflexes and hook behavior clearly, and support explicit git recovery snapshots for book-writing workspaces without silently committing or hiding changes from the author.

source_heading: P1: Reduce enforcement to proportionate durability

text: TYF should capture real author-session hiccups and agent misreads as local, review-only tooling learning candidates so Alexander can write two books while surfacing practical improvements to TYF. Learn from SOLO's local-only fbs learn pattern, but keep TYF independent of SOLO and avoid storing manuscript source text or snippets.

Build the first local TYF book workbench slice: a file-backed Draft Review Workbench where the author can view/edit candidate draft beside the approved manuscript, see book typeface/paragraph style and image asset scaffolding, and prepare selections for the controlled Gate without uncontrolled manuscript writes. Defer print-ready KDP/PDF, full pagination, publisher templates, and full WYSIWYG layout to later work.

TYF's author-facing workbench should evolve beyond a single prompt/chat and a single candidate draft file into a double-surface local authoring cockpit: a full contextual amanuensis chat plus a free editable draft/manuscript work surface. The author should be able to select arbitrary text, ask the amanuensis about that selection, attach author notes to lines/segments, promote notes into footnotes, work across multiple draft/manuscript units, and use a full-book graph to understand cross-chapter/phrase implications of changes. The experience should preserve TYF's hidden apparatus and Gate discipline while reducing the limitations of a non-interactive single text input.

TYF should support a Codex-aware local workbench architecture where the amanuensis can remain available as a Codex skill while a local web UI stays two-way aware of the same book state. The preferred design is a local TYF daemon/event bus that watches the workspace and serves the browser UI; Codex lifecycle hooks publish session/prompt/tool/turn events into that bus; a TYF MCP server exposes current workbench state, selection, notes, book graph, and safe actions to Codex; and, when full in-browser amanuensis chat is desired, the workbench can use Codex app-server or SDK to start/resume/steer a Codex thread and stream agent events. The integration must preserve plain-file source of truth and Gate discipline.
